title: The Science of Happiness
summary: A narrator explores what psychological research has discovered about happiness, examining factors such as relationships, purpose, and gratitude, and questioning whether happiness can truly be pursued or must simply be allowed to emerge.
body: For centuries, philosophers have debated the nature of happiness. Today, psychologists have joined the conversation, and the results of their research are both surprising and encouraging. It turns out that happiness is not simply a matter of having more money, more possessions, or more success. In fact, once our basic needs have been met, additional wealth contributes very little to our overall sense of wellbeing. So what actually makes us happy? One of the most consistent findings in positive psychology is that strong social relationships are the single greatest predictor of long-term happiness. People who have close friends, supportive families, and a sense of belonging to a community report significantly higher levels of life satisfaction than those who live in isolation. This is true across cultures, age groups, and income levels. Another key factor is having a sense of purpose. People who feel that their work or daily activities contribute to something larger than themselves tend to experience what researchers call eudaimonic wellbeing — a deep sense of meaning and fulfilment that goes beyond simple pleasure. Volunteers, caregivers, and creative professionals often report this kind of satisfaction, even when their roles are demanding or financially modest. Gratitude has also emerged as a powerful tool for improving mood. Studies have shown that people who regularly write down things they are grateful for experience lower levels of anxiety and depression. The simple act of noticing what is going right in one's life, rather than focusing on what is lacking, appears to rewire the brain over time. Interestingly, research suggests that we are remarkably poor at predicting what will make us happy. We tend to overestimate the pleasure that major purchases or achievements will bring, and we underestimate how quickly we will adapt to new circumstances — a phenomenon psychologists call hedonic adaptation. This means that the new car, the promotion, or the bigger house feels wonderful at first, but its effect on our happiness fades far more quickly than we expect. Perhaps the most hopeful conclusion from all this research is that happiness is, to a significant extent, a skill. It can be practised through mindfulness, nurturing relationships, acts of kindness, and deliberate reflection. We may not be able to manufacture joy on demand, but we can certainly cultivate the conditions in which it is more likely to grow.

annotations.meaningVi: nhu cầu cơ bản đã được đáp ứng
annotations.exampleEn: Once basic needs have been met, people focus on personal growth.

annotations.meaningVi: cảm giác hạnh phúc tổng thể
annotations.exampleEn: Exercise improves your overall sense of wellbeing.
Positive psychology focuses on strengths rather than disorders.

annotations.meaningVi: cảm giác thuộc về, cảm giác được chấp nhận
annotations.exampleEn: Team sports give children a strong sense of belonging.

annotations.meaningVi: sự hài lòng với cuộc sống
annotations.exampleEn: Researchers measured participants' life satisfaction using a standard scale.

annotations.meaningVi: ý thức về mục đích sống
annotations.exampleEn: Having a sense of purpose helps people overcome difficulties.

annotations.meaningVi: hạnh phúc theo nghĩa phát triển bản thân và ý nghĩa cuộc sống
annotations.exampleEn: Eudaimonic wellbeing comes from living in line with your values.
Expressing gratitude can strengthen personal relationships.
Regular exercise can reduce anxiety and depression.

annotations.meaningVi: tái cấu trúc não bộ, thay đổi thói quen tư duy
annotations.exampleEn: Meditation can rewire the brain to be calmer under stress.

annotations.meaningVi: sự thích nghi với khoái lạc — xu hướng quay về mức độ hạnh phúc ban đầu
annotations.exampleEn: Hedonic adaptation explains why lottery winners are not always happier long term.
People often overestimate how long it will take to finish a task.

annotations.meaningVi: thích nghi với hoàn cảnh mới
annotations.exampleEn: Humans are surprisingly good at adapting to new circumstances.

annotations.meaningVi: chánh niệm, sự tập trung vào hiện tại
annotations.exampleEn: Many companies now offer mindfulness sessions for employees.

annotations.meaningVi: tạo ra điều kiện thuận lợi
annotations.exampleEn: Good teachers cultivate the conditions for students to thrive.

annotations.meaningVi: sự suy ngẫm có chủ đích
annotations.exampleEn: Deliberate reflection after a project helps teams improve.
Random acts of kindness can boost your own mood as well.

grammarNotes.title: Present Perfect Passive — Describing research findings
grammarNotes.bodyVi: Bài dùng thì hiện tại hoàn thành bị động để trình bày các phát hiện nghiên cứu mà không cần chỉ rõ người thực hiện. Ví dụ: 'basic needs have been met', 'Gratitude has also emerged'. Cấu trúc: have/has + been + past participle. Dạng này phổ biến trong văn viết học thuật và báo cáo.
grammarNotes.bodyEn: The present perfect passive is used to present research findings without specifying who performed the research. Structure: have/has + been + past participle. Examples: 'basic needs have been met', 'Gratitude has also emerged'. This construction is common in academic and journalistic writing.

grammarNotes.title: Relative Clauses — Adding detail to nouns
grammarNotes.bodyVi: Bài sử dụng mệnh đề quan hệ xác định và không xác định để bổ sung thông tin cho danh từ. Ví dụ: 'People who have close friends… report higher levels' (xác định — chỉ những người cụ thể). 'a phenomenon psychologists call hedonic adaptation' là mệnh đề quan hệ rút gọn (không có 'which'). Mệnh đề quan hệ giúp câu văn súc tích và phức tạp hơn.
grammarNotes.bodyEn: The passage uses both defining and non-defining relative clauses to add information to nouns. Example: 'People who have close friends… report higher levels' (defining — identifies which people). 'a phenomenon psychologists call hedonic adaptation' is a reduced relative clause (omitting 'which'). Relative clauses make sentences more concise and information-dense.

translationVi:
Trong nhiều thế kỷ, các triết gia đã tranh luận về bản chất của hạnh phúc. Ngày nay, các nhà tâm lý học đã tham gia vào cuộc trò chuyện này, và kết quả nghiên cứu của họ vừa bất ngờ vừa đáng khích lệ. Hóa ra, hạnh phúc không chỉ đơn giản là có nhiều tiền hơn, nhiều tài sản hơn, hay thành công hơn. Thực tế, một khi các nhu cầu cơ bản đã được đáp ứng, sự giàu có thêm đóng góp rất ít vào cảm giác hạnh phúc tổng thể của chúng ta.

Vậy điều gì thực sự khiến chúng ta hạnh phúc? Một trong những phát hiện nhất quán nhất trong tâm lý học tích cực là mối quan hệ xã hội bền chặt là yếu tố dự báo lớn nhất cho hạnh phúc lâu dài. Những người có bạn bè thân thiết, gia đình ủng hộ, và cảm giác thuộc về một cộng đồng báo cáo mức độ hài lòng cuộc sống cao hơn đáng kể so với những người sống cô lập. Điều này đúng ở mọi nền văn hóa, nhóm tuổi và mức thu nhập.

Một yếu tố quan trọng khác là có ý thức về mục đích. Những người cảm thấy công việc hay hoạt động hàng ngày của họ đóng góp vào điều gì đó lớn hơn bản thân thường trải nghiệm điều mà các nhà nghiên cứu gọi là eudaimonic wellbeing — một cảm giác sâu sắc về ý nghĩa và sự viên mãn vượt ra ngoài niềm vui đơn thuần. Các tình nguyện viên, người chăm sóc, và các chuyên gia sáng tạo thường báo cáo loại sự thỏa mãn này, ngay cả khi công việc của họ đòi hỏi nhiều hoặc thu nhập khiêm tốn.

Lòng biết ơn cũng đã nổi lên như một công cụ mạnh mẽ để cải thiện tâm trạng. Các nghiên cứu đã chỉ ra rằng những người thường xuyên ghi lại những điều họ biết ơn trải nghiệm mức độ lo lắng và trầm cảm thấp hơn. Hành động đơn giản là chú ý đến những gì đang diễn ra tốt trong cuộc sống, thay vì tập trung vào những gì còn thiếu, dường như tái cấu trúc não bộ theo thời gian.

Điều thú vị là, nghiên cứu cho thấy chúng ta rất kém trong việc dự đoán điều gì sẽ làm mình hạnh phúc. Chúng ta có xu hướng đánh giá quá cao niềm vui mà những mua sắm hay thành tích lớn sẽ mang lại, và đánh giá thấp việc chúng ta sẽ thích nghi nhanh như thế nào với hoàn cảnh mới — một hiện tượng mà các nhà tâm lý học gọi là sự thích nghi với khoái lạc. Điều này có nghĩa là chiếc xe mới, sự thăng chức, hay ngôi nhà lớn hơn cảm thấy tuyệt vời lúc đầu, nhưng tác động của nó đến hạnh phúc của chúng ta phai mờ nhanh hơn nhiều so với dự đoán.

Có lẽ kết luận hy vọng nhất từ tất cả những nghiên cứu này là hạnh phúc, ở một mức độ đáng kể, là một kỹ năng. Nó có thể được luyện tập thông qua chánh niệm, nuôi dưỡng các mối quan hệ, những hành động tử tế, và sự suy ngẫm có chủ đích. Chúng ta có thể không tạo ra niềm vui theo yêu cầu, nhưng chắc chắn có thể tạo ra những điều kiện trong đó nó dễ nảy sinh hơn.

questions.prompt: According to the passage, what do psychologists say about money and happiness?
options: More money always leads to greater happiness
Money has no effect on happiness at any level
Once basic needs are met, extra wealth adds very little to wellbeing
Wealthy people are always more satisfied with life
questions.explanation: The passage states that 'once our basic needs have been met, additional wealth contributes very little to our overall sense of wellbeing.'
questions.hint: Think about the distinction between having enough and having more.

questions.prompt: What does positive psychology identify as the greatest predictor of long-term happiness?
options: A sense of adventure and novelty
questions.explanation: The passage says 'strong social relationships are the single greatest predictor of long-term happiness.'
questions.hint: Consider what connects people to each other.

questions.prompt: Which groups are mentioned as examples of people who often experience eudaimonic wellbeing?
questions.explanation: The passage specifically mentions 'volunteers, caregivers, and creative professionals' as examples.
questions.hint: These are people whose work involves serving others or creating meaning.

questions.prompt: What effect does regularly writing down things you are grateful for have, according to the passage?
options: It leads to lower levels of anxiety and depression
It makes people more productive at work
It helps people build stronger friendships
questions.explanation: The passage states that people who regularly practise gratitude 'experience lower levels of anxiety and depression.'
questions.hint: The passage connects gratitude to mental health outcomes.

questions.prompt: What does 'hedonic adaptation' mean as described in the passage?
options: The tendency to seek more pleasure over time
The process of adjusting quickly to new positive circumstances so they no longer feel special
A condition that prevents people from feeling happy
The habit of comparing yourself to others
questions.explanation: Hedonic adaptation is described as our tendency to 'adapt to new circumstances' quickly, so the effect of good things 'fades far more quickly than we expect.'
questions.hint: Think about how long a new purchase actually makes you feel happy.

questions.prompt: According to the passage, how bad are people at predicting what will make them happy?
options: They are fairly accurate at predicting happiness
They are only bad at predicting short-term happiness
They tend to overestimate the pleasure that achievements and purchases will bring
They consistently underestimate how happy they will be
questions.explanation: The passage says 'we tend to overestimate the pleasure that major purchases or achievements will bring.'
questions.hint: Think about how you feel after buying something you really wanted.

questions.prompt: What is the passage's main conclusion about happiness?
options: Happiness is determined by genetics and cannot be changed
Happiness is best achieved by setting and reaching major goals
Happiness is a skill that can be cultivated through specific practices
Happiness depends entirely on life circumstances
questions.explanation: The passage concludes that 'happiness is, to a significant extent, a skill' that 'can be practised.'
questions.hint: Look at the final paragraph for the author's main conclusion.

questions.prompt: Which of the following practices is NOT mentioned in the passage as a way to cultivate happiness?
questions.explanation: The passage mentions mindfulness, nurturing relationships, acts of kindness, and deliberate reflection — but not physical exercise.

questions.prompt: How does eudaimonic wellbeing differ from simple pleasure, according to the passage?
options: It is more intense but shorter lasting
It requires more money to achieve
It involves a deep sense of meaning and fulfilment beyond pleasure
It is found mainly in leisure activities
questions.explanation: The passage describes eudaimonic wellbeing as 'a deep sense of meaning and fulfilment that goes beyond simple pleasure.'
questions.hint: The passage contrasts it directly with 'simple pleasure'.

questions.prompt: The passage says the finding about social relationships being the key to happiness is true 'across cultures, age groups, and income levels'. What does this imply?
options: The finding only applies to wealthy societies
The finding is universal and not limited to specific groups
The finding is more relevant to older people
The finding has been challenged by researchers in some countries
questions.explanation: The phrase 'across cultures, age groups, and income levels' means the finding applies universally, to all people.
questions.hint: Think about what 'across' means when followed by a list.

questions.prompt: What does the narrator mean by 'cultivate the conditions in which it is more likely to grow'?
options: Plant gardens to improve mental health
Create environments and habits that make happiness more likely
Wait patiently for happiness to arrive naturally
questions.explanation: The metaphor of cultivation (from gardening) means actively creating the right environment and habits for happiness to develop.
questions.hint: The word 'cultivate' is often used as a metaphor for growing or developing something gradually.

cloze.template: For centuries, philosophers have debated the nature of happiness. Today, psychologists have joined the conversation, and the results of their research are both surprising and encouraging. It turns out that happiness is not simply a matter of having more money, more possessions, or more success. In fact, once our basic needs have been {{b1}}, additional wealth contributes very little to our overall sense of wellbeing. One of the most consistent findings in positive psychology is that strong social {{b2}} are the single greatest predictor of long-term happiness. People who have close friends, supportive families, and a sense of belonging to a community report significantly higher levels of life satisfaction than those who live in {{b3}}. Another key factor is having a sense of {{b4}}. People who feel that their work or daily activities contribute to something larger than themselves tend to experience what researchers call eudaimonic wellbeing — a deep sense of meaning and fulfilment that goes beyond simple pleasure. Gratitude has also emerged as a powerful tool for improving mood. Studies have shown that people who regularly write down things they are {{b5}} for experience lower levels of anxiety and depression. The simple act of noticing what is going right in one's life, rather than focusing on what is lacking, appears to {{b6}} the brain over time. Interestingly, research suggests that we are remarkably poor at predicting what will make us happy. We tend to {{b7}} the pleasure that major purchases or achievements will bring, and we underestimate how quickly we will adapt to new circumstances — a phenomenon psychologists call hedonic {{b8}}. Perhaps the most hopeful conclusion from all this research is that happiness is, to a significant extent, a {{b9}}. It can be practised through mindfulness, nurturing relationships, acts of kindness, and deliberate {{b10}}. We may not be able to manufacture joy on demand, but we can certainly cultivate the conditions in which it is more likely to grow.

blanks.explanation: 'Needs have been met' is the standard collocation — 'met' means the needs have been fulfilled or addressed.
The passage uses 'social relationships' as the specific term for bonds between people.
The passage contrasts those with community connections against 'those who live in isolation' — alone and disconnected.
'Sense of purpose' is the exact phrase used in the passage to describe feeling that one's activities matter.
The passage uses 'things they are grateful for' — 'grateful' is the specific adjective used in context of gratitude practice.
'Rewire the brain' is the metaphor used in the passage to describe neurological change through habitual positive thinking.
The passage uses 'overestimate' — to think something will be better or greater than it actually is.
'Hedonic adaptation' is the technical term used in the passage for the process of getting used to positive changes.
The passage concludes that 'happiness is a skill' — something that can be learned and practised, not just luck.
'Deliberate reflection' is the phrase used — meaning intentional and thoughtful consideration of one's experiences.

criticalThinkingQuestion: The passage argues that happiness is largely a skill that can be practised. Do you agree that happiness is something we can actively cultivate, or do you think it depends more on external circumstances beyond our control? What evidence from your own life supports your view?
